summary.p: #105. a) Tiruvālantuṟai Mahādeva temple; b) on the wall section to the west of the niche of the Goddess, on the northern façade of the ardha-maṇḍapa, lower inscription; c) personally located and read in situ; d) ARE 1895, no. 120; SII 5, no. 681; SII 32, part 2, no. 111; e) 12th regnal year of Kōpparakesarivarman; f) probably Uttamacōḻa (circa 983 A.D.); g) inscription read with G. Vijayavenugopal; h) the first line seems to be an attempt; then it starts on the left side pilaster, over the wall section and on the right side pilaster; for line 5, the pilasters are not engraved.

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)
Conversion to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
svasti śrī koparak
svasti ‡ śrī kopparakecaripaṉmaṟku yāṇṭu 12 ‡ Āvatu
kuṟak tu ‡brahmadeya ciṟupaḻuvūr tiruvālantu ‡ ṟai maha
[devarkku] ‡ Aṭikaḷ paḻuveṭṭaraiyar kaṇṭan cuntira ‡ coḻanar va
[[tta no]] ‡ [t]tāviḷakku Iraṇṭu Iravum pakalum Eriya ca ‡ ntirāti
ttavaṟ Eriya nārāyattāl nicatam Uriya ney E
rikka vaitta cāvāmuvā perāṭu nūṟṟu Eṇpatu ‡ Erikka va
cca nilaiviḷakku Iraṇṭu tirāmuvoṭṭai [U]ca ‡ ra[m] niṟ[ai]
[pa][[nma]][he]
śvara ra
[kṣai] ||
ItuTheitu panmāheheśvara rakṣai is not engraved in the expected order: line 8 is on the eastern side pilaster, at the bottom; lines 9–11 are on the other side, on the pilaster which is on the western side of the inscription.

Fortune! Prosperity! Kōpparak Fortune! Prosperity! This is the 12th year of Kōpparakesarivarman. To Mahādeva of Tiruvālantuṟai of Ciṟupaḻuvūr, a brahmadeya of Kuṉṟakkūṟṟam (kuṟak{{ūṟṟattu}} > kuṉṟakk{{ūṟṟattu}}), Aṭikaḷ Paḻuvēṭṭaraiyar Kaṇṭan Cuntiracōḻanar placed (vatta > vaitta) for two (iraṇṭu) perpetual lamps (nottāviḷakku > nontāviḷakku) to burn (eriya) night and day (iravum pakalum), as long as the sun and the moon endure, to burn (eriya); he put (vaitta) hundred and eighty (nūṟṟu eṇpatu) undying and non-ageing (cāvāmūvā) great goats (perāṭu) to burn (erikka) one uri of ghee (ney) every day (nicatam) by the nārāyam [measure]; for a standing lamp (nilaiviḷakku) to burn (erikka), he placed (vacca > vaitta) two (iraṇṭu) tirāms and three (mū) pairs (oṭṭai > oṟṟai?) of elevated weight (ucaram niṟai?). This is under the protection of the Panmāheśvaras.

Both the editions proposed by SII do not read the panmāheśvara rakṣai on the pilasters and read the following fragment (on the base of the pilaster between the inscription on the left and a fragment on the right) in continuation of the line 7. However, I would present it separately because the script appears to be different, less carefully engraved. Although it probably concerns the same donation, I think it may have been added later.

As per the stone inscription (kalveṭṭu paṭi), three (muṉṟum) metal standing lamps (tarānilaiviḷakku), one copper (ceppu) water-pot (kuṭam), a pure golden house (cempoṉ kūṭam)G. Vijayavenugopal proposes, rightly in my view, to interpret this as a golden platform. to bath (āṭṭukiṉṟa) Āṭavalār (Śiva the dancer); that is to be placed (iṭṭatu).
Edited and translated in , based on autopsy and photographs (by Valérie Gillet, 2016-2018).
This digital edition by Valérie Gillet and Emmanuel Francis.
